title: Why I Started a Financial Services LLC While Working Full Time at Lowe's
excerpt: A resume sitting in an applicant tracking system isn't compounding. A business, even a small one, is. Here's why I launched MCC Solutions before landing a W-2 role.
body:
I work a shift at Lowe's, then I come home and run a licensed financial services business. Most people would call that backwards. I'd call it the only way I knew how to actually get started.

## The Plan Nobody Hands You

I graduated with a degree in Accounting and Finance, GPA 3.32, ready to do exactly what everyone tells you to do: get an entry-level job, build experience, work your way up. That's still the plan, honestly. I'm actively applying to Junior Accountant and AP Coordinator roles right now.

But "apply and wait" isn't a full-time activity. It's a few hours a week of sending resumes into systems that may or may not have a human read them. I had the rest of my time, a degree I'd already paid for, and a set of skills nobody was using yet.

So in 2025, I started MCC Solutions LLC.

## What It Actually Is

MCC Solutions is a licensed financial services firm built around three things I'm actually qualified to do right now, not someday: notary and loan signing services, bookkeeping, and web design.

I'm a New Jersey Notary Public and an NNA Certified Loan Signing Agent, carrying $100,000 in E&O insurance. I hold QuickBooks ProAdvisor Gold certification across Level 1, Level 2, and Payroll. I have active FINRA SIE and Series 30 licenses, with Series 66 scheduled for May 2026. My Enrolled Agent exam is scheduled for July 2026.

None of that required someone else to hire me first. A license is a license whether or not you have a job title attached to it.

## Why Not Just Wait for the "Real" Job

Here's the honest answer: waiting doesn't build anything. A resume sitting in an applicant tracking system isn't compounding. A business, even a small one, does.

Every signing I complete is real experience inside an actual real estate transaction. Every QuickBooks file I clean up is real bookkeeping work. Every certification I earn is something a job posting can't take back from me once I have it. By the time I do land a W-2 role, I'm not walking in as someone who waited around. I'm walking in as someone who's been doing the work the entire time.

## What Building It While Working Full Time Actually Looks Like

There's no version of this where I quietly built a business on the side without it costing something. I work my Lowe's shifts, covering inventory and logistics in the building materials department. Then I do outreach to title companies, follow up on signing platform assignments, and work on client books in whatever hours are left.

It's not glamorous. Most days it's a handful of emails sent before bed, not a breakthrough. But infrastructure compounds quietly. The site you're reading this on, the licenses, the insurance, the certifications — none of that existed eighteen months ago. It exists now because I kept building it in the hours other people spend doing literally anything else after a shift.

## Where This Goes

I'm not under the illusion that MCC Solutions replaces a full-time accounting career on its own, at least not yet. The realistic plan is both: keep building the business, keep pursuing the W-2 role and the credentials that go with it, and let them reinforce each other. A licensed financial services business run by someone actively pursuing an Enrolled Agent credential and FINRA licensing is a different pitch than a 23-year-old's side hustle.

I'm writing about this build in real time, not after the fact, because I think the messy, unglamorous version is more useful to read than the polished one. If you're trying to build something while working a job that has nothing to do with where you're headed, I see you. It's slower than it looks from the outside, and it's still worth doing.
